title: Chapter One: The Offer
content: The coffee has gone cold an hour ago but I keep drinking it, because it cost £4.50 and that's the arithmetic of being broke — you don't waste, you suffer through. The cup is damp against my palm. Outside the café window, rain threads down the glass in that particular London way that makes everything look like it's been crying.
My laptop screen has two tabs open. One of them is the student loan balance: £87,342. The other is a job listing I've read so many times I could typeset it from memory.
**Live-in Companion Required. Scottish Highlands. Private estate. Annual salary: £200,000. Must be comfortable with remote location, limited connectivity, and children.**
I close the loan tab. Not because the number isn't real. Because I've already memorized that too.
The listing stays. I read it again. Fifteen times now, and each time the salary feels less like a number and more like a door — the kind that opens onto something you can't see from the outside.

My flatmate Priya would tell me this is insane. Two hundred grand for childcare? Nobody pays that. Except Priya is working at a pub in Camden and I'm sitting in a café I can't afford, nursing cold flat white and pretending this is a choice and not a last resort.
I apply. I've been ready to apply since the first time I read it. The cover letter took me forty minutes to write last Tuesday. I've been editing it in my head ever since.

The interview is at a private club in Mayfair. Of course it is. I walk past the brass plaque three times before I find the door — no sign, no number, just a green enamel frame around a buzzer that looks like it was installed when Victoria was alive. The first one. The rain has soaked through my jacket and I smell like wet wool and the Thames, which is not the aesthetic Mayfair prefers, but here's the thing about being twenty-six and £87,000 in debt: you stop caring about aesthetics and start caring about survival.
Or at least, that's what I tell myself.

The lobby smells like old money and furniture polish. A woman at the front desk gives me the once-over — yes, the jacket is damp, yes, my boots are from the sale rack at TK Maxx, no, I don't belong here — and then directs me up a staircase that creaks with the specific dignity of things that have been expensive for a very long time.

Henry Wingrave is in a leather booth by the window. He's handsome in the way certain men are handsome after their best years — good bone structure underneath the wear, like a beautiful house that's been let go. Silver at the temples. Blue eyes that would be striking if they'd look at you. He's holding a gin and tonic. The gin smells bright and botanical even from across the room, and there's a second one already sweating in front of the empty seat.
"Miss Bly." He stands. His handshake is dry, firm, and brief. He doesn't hold on. "Please, sit. I took the liberty."
I sit. The gin is in front of me. I don't drink it. Not yet.
"Thank you for meeting me, Mr. Wingrave."
"Henry, please." He smiles. It doesn't reach his eyes. Very few things do, I suspect. "I should warn you — I'm not very good at this. Interviews. I always feel like I'm the one being examined."
He laughs at his own joke. I laugh too, the right amount, the practiced amount. Psychology degree. I know how this works.

I ask about the children. I ask about the estate. I ask about the schedule, the expectations, the living arrangements. All the questions a normal applicant would ask. I'm careful about that — very careful — because the questions I don't ask would fill a ledger.

I don't ask why the salary is three times the going rate. I don't ask why the listing was posted on a niche forum instead of a normal job board. I don't ask about the staff who came before me, except once, obliquely, and that's when it happens.
"I understand there have been previous companions?" I say it lightly. Just curious. Nothing more.
His hand tightens on the gin. Just briefly. Just enough. The ice shifts with a sound like teeth.
"I'm afraid the position has a certain... turnover."
The way he says *turnover* — rehearsed. Polished. A stone that's been tumbled in his mouth so many times the edges are smooth. He still won't meet my eyes.
"People find the isolation difficult," he adds. "The Highlands are not for everyone."
"Isolation doesn't frighten me," I say.
And it doesn't. But that's not what he's really asking, is it?

He pulls out his phone. "You should see the children. Flora and Miles." The way he says their names is tender and distant at once, like he's reading them off a memorial. "They have a presence online. My sister's idea, initially. She manages their account."
He turns the screen toward me. TikTok. @TheAshfordTwins. 10M followers.
I watch.

Two children, pale as porcelain, dressed in clothes that belong in a costume drama — high collars, linen, knee breeches. They're standing against the backdrop of a manor house that makes something in my chest go very still. Grey stone, castellated roofline, a lake in the foreground reflecting sky. Beautiful. Gothic. Exactly the kind of building that could swallow you whole and never spit you out.

The video plays. Flora — the girl — sits on the bank of the lake, braiding wildflowers into Miles's hair. Her fingers move in a slow, practiced rhythm. Miles sits perfectly, unnaturally still for a child, gazing at the water. There's no sound on the video, but someone has laid a music track over it — something orchestral and hollow, like a music box left in an attic.
The comments waterfall beneath.
*GOALS*
*adopt me 😭*
*I want to live there*
*I would die for these children*
So do I, I think. I want to live there. The thought surfaces before I can catch it, and for a moment my face does something I can't control. I smooth it out. Smile. Look up at Henry.
"They're beautiful," I say. The word is inadequate, but that's the point. This is the scene where the applicant is charmed. This is the scene where she says yes.
Henry watches me watch the video. He's checking my reaction. I know this because I'm checking his.
"Flora is nine. Miles is eleven," he says. "Bright. Creative. They spend a great deal of time outdoors."
"Alone?"
"With each other. They're quite close." He pauses. "They need someone who can match their... intensity."
I nod. I could match intensity. I've been matching intensity since I was twelve years old and learned how to smile at people who asked questions I couldn't answer.
He circles back to the salary. People always do when the number is absurd — they revisit it like they're checking their own pulse, making sure they didn't imagine it.

"Two hundred thousand, as listed. Paid quarterly. Accommodation and meals included, of course." He takes a long sip of gin. Then, quieter: "The Highlands can be... isolating. There's limited connectivity. The children are extraordinary, but they require someone with particular resilience."
*Resilience.*
He says it gently. Clinically. The way a doctor says *terminal* — from a safe distance, with practiced compassion, like the word might be contagious if he lets it touch his teeth.
"I'm resilient," I say.

He looks at me — really looks, for the first time since I sat down. His eyes are pale and tired and searching, and I can see him running calculations. Not about whether I'm qualified. About whether I'll last. About whether I'll run, or break, or — something else. Something he doesn't have a word for, or doesn't want to say.
"You seem so," he says finally. "I'll be in touch by the end of the week."
We shake hands again. I leave the gin untouched on the table. The rain has stopped outside, but Mayfair still smells wet — petrichor and privilege and something underneath I can't name.

The train home is delayed, because of course it is. I stand pressed against the doors of the Jubilee Line with someone's elbow in my ribs and the particular fug of commuting Londoners — damp coats, warm phones, the sour-sweet of rain on synthetic fabric. I wait until I'm on the overground, seated by a window, before I open my laptop.
Not the regular browser.
The other one. Private mode. No saved history. No autofill. No trail.
I type: *Bly Manor Peter Quint*

The results load. Local news, five years ago. Regional Scottish paper, the Inverness Courier. The headline is careful and factual in the way small-town journalism is careful and factual when something terrible happens to someone important:
**VALET DIES IN FALL AT SCOTTISH ESTATE**

I read the article. I've read it before. I've read it dozens of times. Peter Quint, 34, employed as personal valet to the Wingrave family, found dead at the base of the manor's east tower following a fall. Police investigated. No evidence of foul play. Case closed.
I scroll past the text to the photograph.
The manor.

Grey stone. Castellated roofline. The lake. It's the same building from the TikTok — the same angle, almost, like the photographer was standing in the same spot the twins' camera would stand five years later. Only in this photo, there's police tape and a cluster of figures at the base of the tower, small as chess pieces.
I zoom in on the manor. The windows are dark. Every single one.

My face in the screen light is not a job applicant's face. The glow turns my skin blue and shadowed, and my eyes have a quality I've seen before in mirrors — the focused, utterly still attention of someone who has been watching a door for a very long time and finally sees it opening.

I close the laptop. The train rattles through Wembley. A man across the aisle is watching a football match on his phone with no headphones. The tinny crowd noise sounds like something else — like a house exhaling.
In my flat, I open the laptop again. The email to Henry Wingrave is already composed. I wrote it on the train, in my head, word by word:

*Dear Mr. Wingrave, Thank you for meeting with me today. I would like to formally accept the position of live-in companion to Flora and Miles. I can begin at your earliest convenience. Warm regards, Maeve Bly.*
My finger hovers over send.
Instead, I open a new tab. Private mode. I type:
*Rebecca Jessel Bly Manor*
Zero results. Not a single one. No news articles. No social media. No records at all. As if the name has been erased — or as if it was never written down in the first place.
I stare at the blank results page. The cursor blinks. One second. Two.
I switch back to the email.
I press send.
The screen goes dark — some glitch, the battery cutting out for a half-second before the backlight recovers. But in that instant of darkness, the screen becomes a mirror. And in the mirror:
My face. Staring back.
Or someone else's.

The laptop hums back to life. The sent confirmation glows in my inbox. Around me, the flat is quiet — Priya won't be home until midnight, and the only sound is the rain starting again against the window and the pulse in my own ears.
I close the laptop slowly.

Outside, London keeps raining. Somewhere four hundred miles north, a manor house stands at the edge of a lake with all its windows dark, and inside it — or underneath it, or behind it, or in the spaces between its walls where the Wi-Fi doesn't reach — something is waiting.
Something, or someone.
I lie on my back on my bed and stare at the ceiling and feel the shape of what I've just done settle over me like deep water. Not dread. Not excitement.
Purpose.

Ten years I've been looking. Ten years of search histories and dead ends and names that led to other names that led to nothing. And now the door is opening, and I'm walking through it, and the only question that matters is the one I can't type into a search bar:
What happened to Rebecca Jessel?
The rain hits the glass. I close my eyes. Tomorrow, I'll pack.
Tonight, I'll dream about a lake and two children who stand too still and a house that watches.
